narrative:
A warm, humid Sunday is underway. An approaching front from the west triggers scattered showers and thunderstorms this afternoon, lingering into the evening. Monday is cooler and less humid with decreasing clouds. I think we can keep that comfortable mountain weather going into Tuesday. Warmer weather follows.

2027 Blue Ridge Cameo Photo Winners Announced

In addition to our 15 winning photographs in the Blue Ridge Photo Contest, check out the 36 "Cameo Photo Winners" on the Photo Contest page .

RaysWeather.Com App

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Hi: 82
raw_text:
Show map Boone Right Now 78.1°F Today's forecast: Increasing clouds; Warm and more humid; Scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms; West wind, becoming 5-15 mph. High 82° Station sponsor Forecast Current Graphics Archives Webcams Warm, Humid, Scattered PM Thunderstorms Last Updated Sun June 14 at 10:04AM by Ray Russell A warm, humid Sunday is underway. An approaching front from the west triggers scattered showers and thunderstorms this afternoon, lingering into the evening. Monday is cooler and less humid with decreasing clouds. I think we can keep that comfortable mountain weather going into Tuesday. Warmer weather follows. 2027 Blue Ridge Cameo Photo Winners Announced In addition to our 15 winning photographs in the Blue Ridge Photo Contest, check out the 36 "Cameo Photo Winners" on the Photo Contest page . RaysWeather.Com App Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android Hi: 82 Sunday Increasing clouds; Warm and more humid; Scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms; West wind, becoming 5-15 mph Lo: 59 Sunday night Mostly cloudy; Widely scattered evening showers and thundershowers; A lingering shower possible after midnight along the western escarpment; West to NW wind 5-15 mph, breezy Hi: 74 Monday Decreasing clouds; Cooler and less humid;; NW wind 5-15 mph, breezy in the morning Lo: 54 Monday night Partly cloudy; Cooler; NW wind, becoming light Hi: 74 Tuesday Partly sunny; Pleasant; Light wind becoming SW 5-10 mph Lo: 54 Tuesday night Partly cloudy; Seasonably cool; Light NW wind Hi: 79 Wednesday Mostly to partly sunny; Seasonable Lo: 60 Wednesday night Becoming partly cloudy; Pleasant Hi: 83 Thursday Increasing afternoon clouds; Warmer and more humid; Scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms Lo: 62 Thursday night Mostly cloudy; Showers and thunderstorms remain possible Friday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Saturday Extended forecast is only available for RaysWeather.com supporters. Subscribe Forecast Discussion A front moves across the mountains from the west tonight, and with a humid air mass in place, expect scattered showers and thunderstorms this afternoon. Storms taper off this evening, though a stray shower may hang along the western escarpment overnight. High pressure builds in behind the front for the first half of the workweek. Monday and Tuesday turn cooler and less humid. The front stalls to our south, with disturbances moving west to east along it. Our forecast keeps the rain to our south, but we have some uncertainty for Tuesday and Wednesday regarding the potential for an afternoon shower. Wednesday will be warmer in a more southerly flow. The next front arrives Friday morning. We will have a surge of warm, humid air ahead of it, producing scattered showers and thunderstorms Thursday afternoon and Thursday night. The front moves through slowly Friday, keeping showers in the area for much of the day. Temperatures will be cooler again Friday. Computer models are picking up a developing low in South Texas this week. We think it will remain far enough to our west to not be a factor in our weather this week, but not all guidance agrees with that assessment, so we have uncertainty by the end of the week. For now, we took the optimistic track for a mostly pleasant Saturday. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. Subscribe Log in Ray Russell Founder & CEO PHOTO OF THE DAY CLICK TO ENLARGE Ehh, what’s up Doc? Submitted by Chris May. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights El Niño Has Officially Arrived El Niño has officially arrived, as described in the latest Climate Prediction Center ENSO discussion. This will have significant implications on fall and winter temperature and precipitation in the Southeastern United States. While our region continues to experience significant drought conditions, above-normal precipitation will ultimately make a comeback during fall and winter. Jun 14, 2026 Monday Message for June 8, 2026 This week's Monday Message highlights a week of hit-or-miss showers and thundershowers that will be returning to the region in typical June fashion. Also, we discuss the movie "Pressure", in theaters now. Jun 08, 2026 May 2026 Recap Dry, Until It Wasn't The story of Spring 2026 was drought. Many stations experienced their warmest and/or driest April on record, and while the warmth backed off some into May, it took a while for the pattern to finally make a move towards the wetter side of things. But once it did, it did so with a bang. Not everywhere in the area made it to their respective May average precipitation numbers, due to the somewhat hit-or-miss nature of the rainy stretch around Memorial Day, but most recieved 70% or more of a month's worth of rainfall in about a week. No station took advantage of the wetter pattern more than the Asheville Airport, south of downtown. In a night that produced flash flooding damage to roadways around NE Henderson, SE Buncombe, and western Rutherford counties, the airport picked up nearly 3 inches of rain. 2.89", to be exact. That is almost as much as North Wilkesboro picked up for the entire month! As for KAVL's monthly total, it was an impressive 8.62", more than doubling average. It rained so much in the final 12 days of the month that the airport went from being over 8 inches behind normal annual accumulation on May 19th, to being just under 2 inches behind schedule. The graph below shows the numbers for the first 5 months of 2026 in green, as well as the wettest (blue) and driest (red) years, along with average (brown). In the High Country and SW Virginia, things were fairly hit-or-miss, with Boone, Beech Mountain, Wytheville, and Banner Elk all falling between 65 and 85 percent of average monthly rainfall, while Galax's 4.94" exceeded average by 24 percent. As mentioned earlier, the vast majority of this fell in roughly a 7 to 10 day stretch to end the month. In Boone's case, most of it fell in the days centered around the gloomy Memorial Day weekend. You can also see that warm stretch in the mid-month period before we finally made the switch to a wetter pattern, highlighted by four straight highs in the low 80s. Memorial Day Weekend Wedge As we get deeper into the summer, the classic Appalachian "wedge" becomes a more infrequent and less impactful phenomenon. However, late spring and summertime wedges are certainly not unheard of, and we had a doozy just before Memorial Day. Remarkably cool high temperatures invaded the Foothills on the 23rd, when many locations recorded a half inch to an inch of rain. The weather whiplash was strong in Wilkesboro, where three straight days in the 90s was suddenly interrupted by 80s, then 60s, then a high of just 55 degrees on the 23rd! The map below shows the daily rainfall for the 23rd. Record April to Average May If you recall from our April Recap, several locations in our region saw the warmest April on record. In Morganton, the average high temperature for the month of April was right under 80 degrees. What makes that number especially remarkable is that not only is it above average for the months of April and May, but that it actually was slightly higher than the average high temperature for May! In fact, the month-to-month increase in average high temperature from April to May should be about 7 or 8 degrees, depending on where you are. None of the sites listed in the tables below increased by more than 2.1 degrees, and four of them actually decreased (Lenoir, Marshall, Morganton, and North Wilkesboro). By and large, the final numbers for May were about as close to average as you will find. With the remarkable warmth of April being fairly close to May averages, we essentially had "two Mays" in 2026, weather-wise. Local Details The tables and maps below re-iterate the story told above, but at a more local scale. Only a handful of outliers exceed ~1.5F anomalies on average temperature for May. While there was plenty of variance in between, the washed-out average is almost exactly where it usually is.

As mentioned before, precipitation was a bit hit-or-miss with the switch over to summertime convective storms versus the widespread precipitation events of the cold season. However, most managed to get within 20% of normal or exceed it. The "feast or famine" is most obvious with the percentage differences from the Asheville Airport to Mount Airy: 203.7% vs 53.2%.
